event: Neanderthals inhabit Gibraltar's caves, with Gorham's Cave preserving some of the last known Neanderthal occupation sites in Europe.
Engravings attributed to Neanderthals, including a crosshatch pattern in Gorham's Cave, are made on the cave's bedrock.
Anatomically modern humans use the caves of Gibraltar during the Mesolithic period.
Phoenician seafarers establish a presence in the region, using nearby Carteia and revering the Rock as one of the Pillars of Hercules.
Berber commander Tariq ibn Ziyad lands at the Rock to launch the Umayyad conquest of Hispania, giving the place its name Jabal Tariq (Tariq's Mountain).
Almohad caliph Abd al-Mu'min orders the first permanent town and fortifications built on the Rock, founding Madinat al-Fath.
Castilian forces under Ferdinand IV capture Gibraltar from the Moors for the first time during the Reconquista.
The Marinid dynasty besieges and retakes Gibraltar for the Moors after a Castilian relief effort fails.
Castilian forces under the Duke of Medina Sidonia conquer Gibraltar definitively from the Moors, ending Muslim rule on the Rock.
A community of converso (New Christian) Jews is briefly settled in Gibraltar before being expelled.
Queen Isabella I of Castile annexes Gibraltar to the Crown of Castile and grants it a coat of arms.
Barbary corsairs raid Gibraltar, carrying off many inhabitants into slavery and prompting stronger Spanish fortifications.
A Dutch fleet under Jacob van Heemskerck destroys a Spanish squadron anchored off Gibraltar during the Eighty Years' War.
An Anglo-Dutch fleet captures Gibraltar from Spain during the War of the Spanish Succession, beginning continuous British control.
The Treaty of Utrecht cedes Gibraltar to Great Britain in perpetuity, a transfer Spain has long contested.
Spain fails to retake Gibraltar in a thirteenth siege during the brief Anglo-Spanish War.
The Great Siege of Gibraltar begins as Spanish and French forces blockade the Rock during the American War of Independence.
The Great Siege ends in British victory after nearly four years, cementing Gibraltar's reputation as an impregnable fortress.
The Battle of Trafalgar is fought off the nearby cape, and Admiral Nelson's body is brought to Gibraltar aboard HMS Victory.
Gibraltar is formally declared a British Crown Colony, establishing its civil administration.
The opening of the Suez Canal greatly increases Gibraltar's strategic importance as a Mediterranean gateway and coaling station.
Construction begins on a major new harbour and dockyard, transforming Gibraltar into a key Royal Navy base.
Britain builds a fence at the frontier with Spain, later a source of dispute over the neutral zone of the isthmus.
Most of Gibraltar's civilian population is evacuated to Britain, Madeira, and Jamaica as the Rock becomes a vital wartime fortress.
Gibraltar serves as the command headquarters for Operation Torch, the Allied invasion of North Africa, with tunnels expanded into a fortress city.
Polish leader General Wladyslaw Sikorski dies in a plane crash just off Gibraltar shortly after takeoff.
Gibraltar's first Legislative Council is established, giving the colony a measure of representative government.
Queen Elizabeth II visits Gibraltar, prompting Spain under Franco to intensify its sovereignty claim and restrict the frontier.
Spain raises the Gibraltar sovereignty dispute at the United Nations, beginning decades of decolonization debate.
Gibraltarians vote overwhelmingly in a referendum to remain under British sovereignty rather than pass to Spain.
A new constitution grants Gibraltar self-government, prompting Franco's Spain to fully close the land frontier.
Gibraltar joins the European Economic Community alongside the United Kingdom as a dependent territory.
Spain partially reopens the frontier to pedestrians, easing the blockade imposed in 1969.
Spain fully reopens the land border with Gibraltar as a condition of its own accession to the European Community.
British special forces kill three IRA members in Gibraltar in Operation Flavius, an incident that sparks international controversy.
The last British infantry battalion withdraws, and the Royal Gibraltar Regiment takes over local defence duties.
Gibraltarians overwhelmingly reject a proposal for shared British-Spanish sovereignty in a referendum.
Gibraltar celebrates the tercentenary of British rule with a tripartite dialogue process opening between Britain, Spain, and Gibraltar.
A new constitution gives Gibraltar greater self-government and is approved by referendum, modernising its relationship with Britain.
Spain's foreign minister makes the first official Spanish ministerial visit to Gibraltar in 300 years under the tripartite forum.
A dispute over an artificial reef triggers heightened tensions and intensive Spanish border checks at the frontier.
Gibraltar votes 96 percent to remain in the European Union, but is bound to leave with the United Kingdom in the Brexit referendum.
Gibraltar leaves the European Union with the United Kingdom at the end of the Brexit transition, ending its EEC/EU membership since 1973.
Britain and Spain reach an in-principle deal for Gibraltar to join the Schengen area's framework, pending a final EU treaty.
